coredata.dc:description: Although several methods for enhancing the selectivity of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been reported, their effects on complex substrates remain unsatisfactory. In this study, a customised 1-vinyl-3-hydroxyethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid with a vinyl double bond polymerizable unit and an F-atom recognition unit was employed as a precursor carbon source to prepare unique spherical carbonised polymer dots (CPDs-F) through an incomplete carbonisation process using the one-step hydrothermal method. Then used it as a novel functional monomer, sulfadiazine sodium (SDS), as a template to synthesize the SDS-imprinted material CPDs-F@MIP in aqueous media. The results indicated that the obtained CPDs-F@MIP exhibited excellent selectivity for the template, with an imprinting factor of up to 6.39, surpassing that of the MIPs prepared with chain functional monomers 2.12–4.76 times. The selective adsorption of the template molecule, SDS (selectivity coefficient: approximately 2.615–43.149), was not significantly influenced by the coexistence of contaminants with various structural characteristics. The maximum adsorption capacity reached 61.06 mg g−1. After at least twenty applications, the adsorption capability of CPDs-F@MIP did not decrease significantly. Finally, the prepared CPDs-F@MIP were used as solid-phase extraction adsorbents in combination with high-performance liquid chromatography, and the anti-interference ability and selective adsorption performance of CPDs-F@MIP in six actual water samples were investigated. Additionally, an accurate, reliable method for detecting SDS in environmental water was established (limit of detection of 0.2 μg L−1, and the recoveries ranged from 91.79% to 111.32%). Imprinting materials prepared using spherical functional monomers significantly enhance their selective recognition ability in complex media and provide new ideas for the preparation and application of novelty imprinting materials with superior performance.

head.ce:title: High selectivity and anti-interference molecularly imprinted polymer based on spheroidal carbonized polymer dots for effective sulfadiazine sodium removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although several methods for enhancing the selectivity of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been reported, their effects on complex substrates remain unsatisfactory. In this study, a customised 1-vinyl-3-hydroxyethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid with a vinyl double bond polymerizable unit and an F-atom recognition unit was employed as a precursor carbon source to prepare unique spherical carbonised polymer dots (CPDs-F) through an incomplete carbonisation process using the one-step hydrothermal method. Then used it as a novel functional monomer, sulfadiazine sodium (SDS), as a template to synthesize the SDS-imprinted material CPDs-F@MIP in aqueous media. The results indicated that the obtained CPDs-F@MIP exhibited excellent selectivity for the template, with an imprinting factor of up to 6.39, surpassing that of the MIPs prepared with chain functional monomers 2.12–4.76 times. The selective adsorption of the template molecule, SDS (selectivity coefficient: approximately 2.615–43.149), was not significantly influenced by the coexistence of contaminants with various structural characteristics. The maximum adsorption capacity reached 61.06 mg g−1. After at least twenty applications, the adsorption capability of CPDs-F@MIP did not decrease significantly. Finally, the prepared CPDs-F@MIP were used as solid-phase extraction adsorbents in combination with high-performance liquid chromatography, and the anti-interference ability and selective adsorption performance of CPDs-F@MIP in six actual water samples were investigated. Additionally, an accurate, reliable method for detecting SDS in environmental water was established (limit of detection of 0.2 μg L−1, and the recoveries ranged from 91.79% to 111.32%). Imprinting materials prepared using spherical functional monomers significantly enhance their selective recognition ability in complex media and provide new ideas for the preparation and application of novelty imprinting materials with superior performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides were the first artificial chemicals used as antibacterial medications. The wide-ranging bacteriostatic efficacy of these compounds against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria has resulted in their global use in both human and animal medicine [1–3]. Sulfadiazine sodium (SDS) belongs to the class of sulfonamides. It is often used to treat of bacterial infections, including urinary tract infections, lung infections, and certain skin diseases. Nevertheless, SDS may induce adverse effects, such as allergic reactions and disruption of the gut microbiota, which can lead to diarrhea, bloating, and other digestive problems [4,5]. Additionally, antibiotic abuse and overuse may result in the development of antibiotic resistance; however, it has been discovered that SDS is carcinogenic and has a long half-live [6]. These antibiotics enter the environment through various routes, including agricultural runoff, sewage treatment facilities, and animal manure. Therefore, SDS is potentially detrimental to both human and ecosystems [7]. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and HPLC-mass spectrometry remains the primary methods for quantifying trace SDS residue in complex samples. However, because of its low concentrations and considerable matrix interference, pre-separation and enrichment are essential for the precise quantification of target analytes in complicated real-world samples. Solid-phase extraction (SPE) is often used among the different sample pre-treatment methods owing to its straightforwardness, speed, and superior enrichment efficiency [8]. However, the conventional SPE adsorbents lack selectivity. Consequently, it is essential to design a distinct material with high selectivity as a sorbent for SPE.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which are used as SPE adsorbents for separation and enrichment, have found widespread application because of their high specific recognition ability through template-created cavities [9–11]. For example, Han et al. [12] synthesized a novel sulfamethoxazole molecularly imprinted polymers utilized magnetic metal–organic frameworks/graphene oxide composite as carriers, and methacrylic acid as the functional monomer. The polymers exhibited maximum adsorption capacities, equilibrium adsorption times, and imprinting factors for sulfonamide drugs of 42.65 mg g−1, 120 min, and 1.61, respectively. Cui et al. [13] synthesized a magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MMIPs) with enhanced selectivity were synthesized using sulfamethazine as template molecule, methacrylic acid as the functional monomer and hydrophilic triethylene glycol dimethacrylate as crosslinker. MMIPs could achieve efficient adsorption and preferred binding ability for template SMZ (selectivity factor 1.9). Although MIPs synthesized using conventional functional monomers exhibit improved selectivity toward specific targets compared to traditional adsorption materials [14,15]. Nonetheless, the spatial orientations of the functional groups in these traditional linear functional monomers vary, leading to a spatial resistance that hinders proper matching with the template molecules [16–18]. Consequently, monomers that cannot form effective complexes with monomer-template molecules may exhibit disorganised organisation or self-polymerisation, causing the emergence of non-imprinted regions in MIPs [19,20]. The non-imprinted regions in the imprinted material include active groups that can adsorb coexisting targets and contaminants in a non-specific manner. This leads to a decrease in the recognition specificity of the MIPs. The inaccuracy of the imprinted sites and the presence of non-imprinted regions can lead to a large amount of non-specific adsorption, thus limiting the wide application of MIPs [9,21,22]. Therefore, the development of MIPs that maintain high selectivity in complex systems is a major challenge. Functional monomers are crucial for the preparation of MIPs, as they form complexes with templates to form recognition sites in the polymers [23–25]. The binding strength and degree of matching between functional monomers and templates affect the affinity of the polymer and determine the accuracy and selectivity of its recognition site [26,27]. Therefore, employing novel functional monomers in polymeric systems to create imprinted sites precisely and densely while minimising non-imprinted cavities might be a promising strategy for enhancing the specific recognition capability of MIPs, even in complex systems.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) is a specific type of carbon-based nanomaterial. In contrast to other carbon nanomaterials, CPDs consist of sp 2/sp 3 hybridised carbon atoms with abundant functional groups or organic polymers on their surfaces [28,29]. To date, most of the research on CPDs has focused on the use of conjugated MIPs as advanced nanomaterials or fluorescent nanosensors for the selective identification of analytes in the environment [30,31]. Specific CPDs can be constructed by incorporating recognition units capable of interacting with the target and polymerisation units participating in the aggregation reaction onto the CPDs [32]. Moreover, owing to the unique structure of CPDs, rich functional groups with consistent spatial orientation in functional monomers can fully bind to template molecules [33]. Additionally, the spherical structures of CPDs create spatial confinement, leading to the effective formation of dense, ordered, and accurately imprinted cavities. These cavities inhibit the formation of non-imprinted regions [22,34,35], thereby overcoming non-specific adsorption and enhancing the specific recognition performance of imprinted materials [18]. Therefore, CPDs may be considered the optimal choice of new functional monomers for the preparation of imprinting materials that exhibit exceptional recognition performance.

SDS is a compound characterised by −SO2, −NH2, −NH, and cyclic structures. It readily forms bonds with CPDs containing potent hydrogen-bonding functional groups, such as −F and hydroxyl, through non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces [34]. Furthermore, the nano-spherical structure of CPDs overcomes spatial hindrance, enabling functional groups with a consistent spatial orientation on their surface to bind precisely to SDS, thereby forming dense and ordered imprinting cavities within the polymer. Simultaneously, this combination effectively minimises the generation of non-imprinted regions. Consequently, the resulting imprinted material can more accurately discern the target in complex systems [22,34,35,37], thereby significantly enhancing the selectivity of the imprinted polymer.

In this study, a customised 1-vinyl-3-hydroxyethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid with vinyl double bond polymerisation and F–atom functional groups was employed as a precursor carbon source. A unique carbonised polymeric dot (CPD-F) was developed via cross-linking and incomplete carbonisation. Subsequently, utilising it as a novel functional monomer, SDS served as a template, polystyrene-divinylbenzene (PS-DVB) served as a carrier, and N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide was used as a crosslinking agent to fabricate an SDS-imprinted polymer (CPDs-F@MIP) in aqueous media. The structure and morphology of the CPDs-F@MIP were thoroughly examined, and its adsorption characteristics and potential adsorption processes were investigated. Furthermore, the applicability of CPDs-F@MIP as an SPE adsorbent to selectively adsorb SDS from actual samples was explored.
See Supplementary Material.
See Supplementary Material.

In summary, 1-vinyl-3-hydroxyethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid (1.120 g, 0.005 mol) was combined with 10 mL of deionised water. After undergoing ultrasonication for a duration of 10 min, the mixture was thereafter transferred to a teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 50 mL. It was then heated for 12 h at temperatures of 120, 150, 180, 200, and 220°C, respectively. After the autoclave cooling to ambient temperature, the resultant brown aqueous solution containing the CPDs-F was purified using a 0.22 μm membrane. Dialysis was employed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for approximately 12 h to remove unreacted precursors. Consequently, the CPDs-F solutions were obtained and could be used directly. Fig. 1 A presents a schematic diagram of the synthesis of CPDs-F.

The imprinted material preparation procedure was as follows: SDS (template molecule) (0.0680 g, 0.25 mmol), 600 μL of CPDs-F (functional monomer), 0.15 g of polystyrene-divinylbenzene (carrier), and 20 mL of ultrapure water were dispersed into a 100 mL polyethylene reaction vessel, followed by prepolymerization at 30°C for 4 h. Subsequently, N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (crosslinker) (0.1930 g, 1.25 mmol) and azobisisobutyronitrile (initiator) (10 mg) were dissolved in the solution, which was then deoxygenated using nitrogen for 10 min. The mixture was polymerised in a 60°C water bath for 24 h. The template molecule was subsequently removed from the resulting product via Soxhlet extraction using a 9:1 (v/v) mixture of methanol and acetic acid. Finally, CPDs-F@MIP was obtained after 24 h of vacuum drying at 60°C. The preparation process for CPDs-F@NIP mirrors that for CPDs-F@MIP, with the exception that the SDS template is omitted. Fig. 2 A illustrates a schematic of the preparation of CPDs-F@MIP.

The kinetic behaviour of SDS adsorption on CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP was thoroughly explored. Initially, 10 mg of either CPDs-F@MIP or CPDs-F@NIP was dissolved in 10 mL of SDS aqueous solution exhibiting various concentrations (10 , 20 , and 50 mg L−1). The sample was agitated in a water bath at a temperature of 25°C for certain time intervals (1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 55, 70, 90, 110, 135, 160, 200, and 240 min). The content of SDS in the supernatant was measured by HPLC (refer to Supplementary Material). For the calculation of adsorption capacity, the following equation was used Eq.1 [38]. The imprinting factor (IF) was determined using Eq.2, and the theoretical maximum IF was calculated based on the optimal fitting of the adsorption isotherm with the Qmax value of the adsorption model [39]:

To investigate the adsorption isotherms, 10 mg of as-prepared imprinted polymers were dispersed in 10 mL of an SDS aqueous solution. Initial concentrations ranged from 0.5 to 200 mg L− 1, and the mixture was incubated for 120 min at 25, 35, and 45°C. Upon achieving adsorption equilibrium, the residual solution was subjected to filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane, followed by determination utilising HPLC.

SDS, ciprofloxacin (CIP), levofloxacin (LEF), oxytetracycline (OTC), pefloxacin (PEF), norfloxacin (NOF), doxycycline (DC), tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol B (BPB), carbamazepine (CBZ), and atrazine (ATZ) were selected as reference compounds to assess the selectivity of CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP via adsorption experiments involving single and competitive selectivity. The chemical structures of the substrates investigated in this study are shown in the Supplementary Materials (Fig. S1). Accordingly, 10 mg of either CPDs-F@MIP or CPDs-F@NIP was introduced to 10 mL of a 20 mg L− 1 aqueous solution of SDS, OTC, TC, CTC, DC, CIP, LEF, PEF, NOF, or BPA. In a competitive selectivity adsorption experiment, 10 mg of CPDs-F@MIP or CPDs-F@NIP was added to 10 mL of a 20 mg L− 1 mixture containing SDS and four interfering substances (OTC, CIP, LEF, and NOF). The mixtures were agitated for 2 h in a water bath maintained at 25°C. The subsequent residual concentrations were quantified using HPLC.

Six water samples (tap, lake, river, well, and inlet/outlet water from a sewage treatment plant) sourced from Xinxiang, Henan Province, were selected to evaluate the anti-interference and selectivity of CPDs-F@MIP as an SPE adsorbent within the complex matrices of actual samples (refer to the Supplementary Material for details). In particular, for subsequent use, 100 mL of each water sample was filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane. Subsequently, 10 mL of the aforementioned pre-treated water sample was employed either as a blank or spiked with SDS at concentrations of 10, 20, and 50 μg L−1, respectively.

A thorough investigation was conducted to optimise the adsorption and recognition capabilities of imprinted polymers, meticulously examining various parameters: the preparation temperature of CPDs-F (120–220°C), the ratio of template molecules to functional units (1:100 to 1:1000 μL), the ratio between template molecules and interconnectors (1:0 to 1:20), and the carrier dose (0:10–0.30 g). Data listed in Tables S1–S4 indicate that the optimal synthesis conditions for CPDs-F@MIPs are attained when the mass molar ratio of the template molecule to CPDs-F and the crosslinker is established at 1:600 μL:5, thereby achieving a peerless adsorption capacity (Q, 10.31 mg g−1) and imprinting factor (IF, 4.58). These metrics significantly surpass those obtained with traditional functional monomers, such as MAA@MIP (Q, 4.46 mg g−1; IF, 1.08), AA@MIP (Q, 5.07 mg g−1; IF, 1.22), and AMPS@MIP (Q, 3.80 mg g−1; IF, 0.91), as illustrated in Table S5. The chain-like architecture of conventional monomers, in conjunction with the varied spatial orientation of their binding groups within the polymerisation system, impedes efficacious binding to template molecules. Consequently, the resultant imprinted sites exhibit significant gaps, leading to imprecise molecular targeting and a tendency for chain monomers to undergo self-crosslinking. This results in spatially expanded and ineffective imprinted sites. In contrast, CPDs-F, owing to its higher density of recognition groups and distinctive zero-dimensional structure, enables the functionalised spherical CPDs-F to interact with template molecules in any conceivable direction and angle. This results in the formation of more precise imprinted sites and the minimisation of non-imprinted regions. Thus, the imprinted materials prepared using CPDs-F as a functional monomer exhibited markedly enhanced affinity and selectivity.

The microstructure of CPDs-F, across a temperature range of 120–220°C, was explored through the XPS spectra presented in Fig. 1B. The full XPS spectrum exhibited five characteristic peaks, manifesting at energies of 193.0, 286.0, 402.0, 532.0, and 686.0 eV, which correspond to B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s, and F1s, respectively [39,40]. The composition of CPDs-F-120 (Fig. 1B-i) primarily consisted of C (48.18%), N (9.26%), O (20.88%), and F (13.62%). Notably, elevations in the carbonisation temperature caused alterations in the atomic proportions of each element in the subsequent CPDs-F, diverging from those observed in CPDs-F-120. For example, CPDs-F-220 (Fig. 1B-v) encompassed C (57.79%), N (8.94%), O (10.78%), and F (15.65%). The elevated carbon content of CPDs-F-220 indicates the occurrences of dehydration, condensation, and cross-linking within the reactor.

In the FT-IR spectra depicted in Fig. 1C, the stretching vibration of C–OH is observable at 3554 cm−1, the vibrational absorption band of C=C is observable at 1655 cm−1, the bending vibration of N–H is observable at 1450 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of C–F is observable at 1171 cm−1 [41]. These spectral features suggest that incomplete carbonisation enables the retention of the polymeric unit (C=C) and the functional unit (–F) while concurrently facilitating the formation of a spherical structure. This culminates in the emergence of a novel structure for the functional monomer CPDs-F.

The X-ray diffraction pattern displays the characteristic peak of a typical CPDs-F centred around 2θ = 20◦, which is attributed to polymer chains and highly disordered carbon (Fig. 1D) [42]. Additionally, TEM images (Fig. 1E) revealed that the CPDs-F are uniformly distributed nanodots with a particle diameter of 2.70 ± 0.03 nm. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM images in Fig. 1F demonstrated that the CPDs-F display a lattice-fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the lattice spacing of the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. This confirmed that CPDs-F possesses the characteristic structure of carbon dots [36]. Moreover, photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 1G) revealed that the synthesised CPDs-F exhibited a conspicuous excitation-independent multicolour emission feature. The 1-vinyl-3-hydroxyethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquids did not exhibit nanodot structures or photoluminescence. Therefore, both the structure and properties underwent significant changes before and after the carbonisation of the carbon sources. These notable structures and photoelectric chemical properties further validate the successful synthesis of CPDs-F.

SEM was utilised to analyse the surface morphologies of PS-DVB, CPDs-F@MIP, and CPDs-F@NIP. As depicted in Fig. 2B, the PS-DVB carrier exhibited a spherical shape with a smooth surface and an approximate diameter of 100 μm. Conversely, while the dimensions of CPDs-F@MIP (Fig. 2C) and CPDs-F@NIP (Fig. 2D) did not exhibit significant alterations, the carrier surface manifested increased roughness, and the imprinting layer became distinctly visible. This phenomenon signified that CPDs-F, serving as a functional monomer, was integrally involved in the formation of the imprinting layer and was successfully polymerised on the surface of the PS-DVB carrier. This was further corroborated by XPS analysis. Characteristic peaks were observed at 281.0 and 529.0 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively, in the full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 2E. However, in contrast to the carrier PS-DVB, CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP exhibited distinct signals at 400.1 and 686.0 eV, representing N1s and F1s, respectively [40]. This outcome further substantiates the active engagement of CPDs-F in the polymerisation reaction, ultimately culminating in the successful formation of an imprinted layer on the surface of the carrier.

The thermal stabilities of PS-DVB, CPDs-F@MIP, and CPDs-F@NIP were assessed in a nitrogen environment. As depicted in Fig. 2F, PS-DVB demonstrated commendable stability below 400°C, while CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP exhibited a minor weight loss of approximately 7.83 wt% below 350°C, potentially attributable to the removal of water and small molecules. Above 400°C, the loss of mass notably increased to approximately 74.98 wt%, possibly owing to the structural collapse at these elevated temperatures. The mass losses of CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP occurred earlier than that of PS-DVB, confirming the presence of a polymer layer in CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP [43]. The aforementioned results indicate that CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP maintain commendable thermal stability at temperatures below 350°C.

The specific surface areas of PS-DVB, CPD-F@MIP, and CPD-F@NIP were meticulously evaluated using N2 adsorption–desorption assays, and the results are shown in Fig. 2G and Table S6. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas, pore volumes, and pore diameters of PS-DVB, CPD-F@MIP, and CPD-F@NIP were quantified as 548.2 m2 g−1, 0.5623 cm3 g−1, and 4.10 nm; 204.1 m2 g−1, 0.2402 cm3 g−1, and 4.71 nm; and 218.9 m2 g−1, 0.2396 cm3 g−1, and 4.38 nm, respectively. Although PS-DVB possesses a high specific surface, pore volume and pore diameters, these cavities are ineffective for the adsorption of SDS due to the lack of interacting groups with the template molecule SDS. Therefore, the carrier PS-DVB did not exhibit adsorption for SDS (Table S7). The BET surface areas of CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP were discernibly less compared to that of PS-DVB. this phenomenon can be attributed to the presence of CPDs-F and N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide during the polymerization process on the PS-DVB surface.The formation of the imprinting layer may lead to the filling of certain micropores, resulting in an overall decrease in the specific surface area and pore volume of the newly formed imprinted material. Notably, the pore volume of CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP is notably lower compared to that of PS-DVB, while the pore diameters remain relatively similar. This observation suggests the formation of a novel imprinting layer with distinct imprinting cavities. Such a configuration enhances the accessibility of the imprinted sites, facilitating the mass transfer of the template molecule and reducing the adsorption equilibrium time. In the case of CPDs-F@NIP, effective imprint cavities cannot be formed due to the absence of a template. Conversely, for CPDs-F@MIP, these cavities exhibit improved compatibility with the template, thereby enhancing the selectivity of the imprinted material.

The adsorption kinetics of CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP towards SDS at concentrations of 10, 20, and 50 mg L−1 were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the adsorption rate of SDS initially increased significantly at various initial concentrations, subsequently decelerating over time and ultimately reaching a plateau. The corresponding Qt values were found to be 5.65, 9.78, and 19.87 mg g−1, respectively. For different concentration of SDS (10, 20, 50 mg L−1), adsorption equilibrium can be achieved within 90 min, while for lower concentrations of SDS (<20 mg L−1), adsorption equilibrium can be achieved within 20 min, which is beneficial for practical application. The kinetic adsorption curves of SDS on CPDs-F@NIP exhibited similar trends; however, the SDS adsorption capacity of CPDs-F@NIP was lower. The Qt values at 10, 20, and 50 mg L−1 were only 1.50, 2.21, and 4.43 mg g−1, respectively, which are significantly lower than the adsorption capacity corresponding to CPDs-F@MIP. Additionally, the time required to reach the adsorption equilibrium was longer for CPDs-F@NIP, exceeding 80 min at each tested concentration. The experimental results indicated that the use of spherical CPDs-F as a functional monomer significantly enhanced the selectivity and mass transfer rate of the imprinted materials.

Additionally, the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics and Elovich models (see Supplementary Material) were utilised to assess the adsorption data. Table 1 summarises the fitted kinetic parameters. The adsorption process of SDS by CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP was better suited to the Elovich-order model than to the other two models, as indicated by the higher adjusted correlation coefficient (R2 adj, 0.9233–0.9914) and lower standard deviation (SD, 0.1154–0.6688) of the three models tested. This suggests that the adsorption process is chemically controlled and that the adsorbent surface is energetically heterogeneous. The parameter k
3 , indicative of the initial adsorption rate, serves as a pivotal metric for assessing the immediacy of adsorptive processes. CPDs-F@MIP exhibited k
3 values ranging from 1.0489 to 2.2483, and CPDs-F@NIP exhibited k
3 values ranging from 0.2866 to 0.6689, which not only underscores the superior adsorptive efficacy of CPDs-F@MIP relative to CPDs-F@NIP, but also illuminates its potential applicability in scenarios where the rapidity of adsorption is crucial. This difference in the k
3 values indicates that CPDs-F@MIP has a faster adsorption rate than CPDs-F@NIP, indicating its capability for rapid adsorption applications in actual environmental samples.

Fig. 3B illustrates the adsorption isothermal fitting curves of imprinted polymers towards SDS at temperatures of 25, 35, and 45°C, respectively. The curves indicate that the adsorption capacity of CPDs-F@MIP for SDS increases with increasing initial solution concentration within the experimental concentration range. Conversely, the SDS adsorption capacity of CPDs-F@NIP was notably decreased and exhibited a gradual increase in response to variations in the substrate concentration. At an initial SDS concentration of 200 mg L−1, the maximum SDS adsorption capacities of CPDs-F@MIP at temperatures of 25, 35, and 45°C were 43.64, 47.67, and 50.77 mg g−1, respectively. Remarkably, these values were approximately 5.20, 4.90, and 4.02 times higher than those of CPDs-F@NIP, which were 8.40, 9.75, and 12.64 mg g−1, respectively. The findings showed that CPDs-F@MIP exhibited several sites that closely resembled the molecular structure of the template, indicating its exceptional ability to recognise SDS. In contrast, CPDs-F@NIP did not exhibit any distinct structural sites, resulting in very poor adsorption capacity.

The adsorption data were further analysed using the Langmuir, Freundlich, and Liu adsorption isotherm models (Supplementary Material). The fitted kinetic parameters are summarised in Table 2 . The K
F values for CPDs-F@MIP ranged from 2.2352 to 4.0467, significantly exceeding those of CPDs-F@NIP, which ranged from 0.6759 to 0.9004. This discrepancy indicates that CPDs-F@MIP exhibited a notably higher adsorption capacity and greater affinity for the template molecules. Moreover, the parameter 1/n
F in Freundlich model represents the heterogeneity factor and the adsorption intensity. The value of 1/n
F > 0.1 represents a favorable adsorption condition, while within the range of 0.1 to 1, it indicates that there is suitable interaction between adsorbate and adsorbent, which is more conducive to adsorption [41,44]. Herein, the 1/n
F values for CPDs-F@MIP were within 0.5165–0.5943, which were higher than that of CPDs-F@NIP (0.4516–0.5681), indicating that there was a relatively strong adsorption intensity and favorable interaction between CPDs-F@MIP and SDS. The Liu model exhibited the highest R2 adj values (0.9780–0.9978) and lower standard deviations (SD, 0.5619–1.9312), clearly demonstrating that the Qe fitted by the Liu model most closely matched the experimental data. The theoretical maximal adsorption capacity (Qe ) of SDS onto CPDs-F@MIP was 61.06 mg g−1, while that of CPDs-F@NIP was only 9.55 mg g−1. The imprinting factor, maintaining a range between 5.03 and 6.39 across a temperature range of 25 to 45°C, underscores the consistent affinity and selectivity of the material across varied operational temperatures, thereby emphasising its robustness and extensive applicability in practical applications.
Table 2.

SDS adsorption onto CPDs-F@MIP was examined at temperatures of 25, 35, and 45℃. The evaluation was based on the values of ΔG
0 , ΔH
0 , and ΔS
0 , which are available as Supplementary Materials. The results are presented in Table S8. Overall, the absolute value range of ΔG
0 for physical adsorption was found to be from 0 to 20 kJ mol−1, which was significantly smaller than the corresponding range of 80 to 200 kJ mol−1 for chemisorption [45]. In this investigation, each value of ΔG
0 was negative and exhibited a decrease with increasing temperature, signifying the process of adsorbing SDS onto CPDs-F@MIP is spontaneous and viable. Conversely, the absolute ΔG
0 values for CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP were within the ranges of 20.39–23.47 kJ mol−1 and 18.83–21.17 kJ mol−1, respectively. This suggests that physical and chemical adsorption mechanisms were involved in the adsorption of SDS onto these materials [46,47]. The positive values of ΔH
0 and ΔS
0 indicate that the adsorption process is endothermic and that there is an increase in entropy, respectively. As |ΔH
0 | > |ΔS
0 |, the interaction between SDS and CPDs-F@MIP was determined to be an enthalpy-driven process, with hydrogen bonding as the primary driving force [48].

The single selectivity experiment, as depicted in Fig. 3C, demonstrated that CPDs-F@MIP exhibited a notable adsorption capacity for SDS, achieving 9.78 mg g−1. In contrast, its adsorption capacity for other non-target pollutants, including LEV, PEF, TC, CIP, NOF, OTC, CTC, BPA, BPA, and DC, was significantly attenuated, ranging between 0.98 and 3.80 mg g−1. Moreover, CPDs-F@NIP exhibited a markedly decreased adsorption capacity towards all tested substrates, ranging between 0.33 and 1.50 mg g−1.

The competitive selectivity experiment, depicted in Fig. 3D, unveiled the superior binding capacity of CPDs-F@MIP for SDS (4.60 mg g−1) within a mixed solution, which was over 3.4 times that of other coexisting compounds (＜1.35 mg g−1). In contrast, the binding capacity of CPDs-F@NIP for all the tested targets was very poor, ranging from 0.03 to 1.26 mg g−1. Furthermore, the selectivity parameter K
d values for CPDs-F@MIP for SDS (0.299 L g−1) were substantially greater than those of the reference compounds LEV (0.114 L g−1), NOF (0.044 L g−1), CIP (0.007 L g−1), and OTC (0.009 L g−1) (Table S9). Additionally, the greater the K value, the greater is the selectivity of the materials. In this study, the K values of LEF, NOF, CIP, and OTC for CPDs-F@MIP were 2.615, 6.856, 43.149, and 12.042, respectively, indicating high recognition capability. CPDs-F, with its unique spherical shape and zero-dimensional dimensions, provided precise and abundant binding sites in the synthesised MIPs, simultaneously minimising the occurrence of non-imprinted regions and ensuring accurate alignment with the template molecules. Consequently, the applicability of CPDs-F@MIP in complex systems was improved, greatly expanding the application of MIPs in practical environmental samples.

Given that industrial effluent and natural water often have intricate compositions that might impact the adsorption of SDS [49], the impact of coexisting ions, namely, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Cl−, PO4 2−, CO3 2−, NO3 2−, and SO4 2−, on the removal of SDS by CPDs-F@MIP was explored (refer to Supplementary Material). As illustrated in Fig. 3E, even when coexisting ions were present at concentrations up to 100 times higher than that of SDS, no discernible impact on the removal of SDS was observed, thereby highlighting the remarkable selectivity and anti-interference capability of CPDs-F@MIP. The results suggest that using a spheroidal functional monomer leads to a more compact structure and fewer non-imprinted regions in CPDs-F@MIP. This results in increased resistance to coexisting ions and improves the ability to selectively recognise target pollutants in practical applications.

The performance of CPDs-F@MIP was assessed and contrasted with that of other MIPs produced utilising traditional chain functional monomers, as seen in Table 3
[12,13,50–54]. The CPDs-F@MIP exhibited an imprinting factor of 6.39 and an adsorption capacity of 61.06 mg g−1. The values obtained in this study were much higher than those reported in earlier studies for both the imprinting factor (ranging from 1.34 to 3.01) and the adsorption capacity (ranging from 5.73 to 42.65 mg g−1). Furthermore, the adsorption equilibrium of CPDs-F@MIP was achieved in only 20 min. This was significantly faster than the equilibrium times of traditional adsorbents reported in the literature (Table 3). Additionally, CPDs-F@MIP demonstrated excellent recycling capabilities, sustaining a minimum of twenty cycles, surpassing other MIPs that could only be recycled for fewer than eight cycles. Additionally, CPDs-F@MIP was synthesised in an environmentally friendly aqueous solution, whereas MIPs using conventional functional monomers, such as MAA, MAPDIA, and 4-VP, are often generated in volatile organic solvents that pose risks to both human health and the environment. Therefore, the use of the spherical functional monomer CPDs-F in this study not only increases the efficiency of MIPs but also boosts their applicability as an environmentally friendly adsorbent.

Additionally, the performance of CPDs-F@MIP as an SPE sorbent was evaluated through comparative analysis with commercially available SPE sorbents, including CNWBOND Si-SPE, CNWBOND LC-C18-SPE, CNW Poly-Sery-HLB-SPE, and Oasis Waters-SPE. This assessment focused on the selective adsorption of SDS from aqueous solutions containing various interfering substances. To achieve this, a 10 mL mixture containing 10 mg L−1 of SDS, CBZ, BPA, ATZ, and BPB was passed through different SPE columns at a flow rate of 0.8 mL min−1. Subsequently, the effluents were collected and determined by HPLC. The results, which are presented in Fig. 3F, indicated that the CNWBOND Si-SPE columns exhibited low adsorption capacities and selectivities. The CNWBOND LC-C18-SPE columns demonstrated a moderate adsorption capacity for the target analyte; however, their selectivity was compromised because of the substantial adsorption of interfering compounds. Both the CNW Poly-Sery-HLB-SPE and the Oasis Waters-SPE columns displayed considerable adsorption capacities. However, their lack of selectivity was evident, as they retained all compounds indiscriminately. In contrast, the CPDs-F@MIP column exhibited selective adsorption of SDS, effectively discriminating it from other interfering substances. These results suggest that MIPs synthesised with CPDs-F as the functional monomer offer enhanced recognition capabilities for the target analyte compared to both commercial columns and traditional imprinted polymers prepared with chain functional monomers.

To better understand the adsorption process, XPS measurements were conducted on CPDs-F@MIP both before and after the adsorption of SDS. In the full spectrum (Fig. 4 A) and high-resolution S2p (Fig. 4B), the characteristic S2p peak, observed at 168.00 eV after adsorption, is attributed to the O=S=O bond present in SDS, thereby indicating the successful adsorption of SDS [55]. High-resolution C1s analyses of CPDs-F@MIP were conducted both before (Fig. 4C) and after (Fig. 4D) SDS adsorption. Peaks in the C1s spectra, with binding energies of 284.71, 285.43, 286.61, and 287.85 eV, were attributed to C=C, C–N, C=N, and C=O, respectively. Subsequent to SDS adsorption, these binding energies were observed to be 284.69, 285.48, 286.70, and 287.93 eV, respectively. The peak areas corresponding to C=C, C–N, C=N, and C=O shifted from 59.22% to 58.13%, 5.47% to 7.58%, 14.47% to 19.75%, and 20.84% to 14.54%, respectively. The augmentation of the C=N and C–N peak areas induced by SDS adsorption signified the successful adsorption of SDS, while the variations in peak shifts further underscored the occurrence of the adsorption process [56]. These findings suggest that species of C=C, C–N, C=N, and C=O might be involved in the adsorption of SDS, potentially due to the hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between SDS and CPDs-F [57]. As depicted in Fig. 4E, distinct new peaks emerged at 1262, 1415, and 1581 cm−1 following adsorption. These were attributed to –SO2, C=C, and N–H in SDS, respectively [8,58]. A significant shift of the C–F peak from 1108 to 1121 cm−1 further substantiates the aforementioned hydrogen bonding interactions.

To further elucidate the adsorption mechanism, the influence of pH and the zeta potential of CPDs-F@MIP were examined. The pH is pivotal in the adsorption process, impacting the properties of both the target substance and CPDs-F@MIP. As illustrated in Fig. 4F, CPDs-F@MIP exhibited notable stability within the pH range of 6–9. The SDS adsorption capacities of CPDs-F@MIP and CPDs-F@NIP initially increased and subsequently decreased as the pH increased from 3 to 12. Simultaneously, the zeta potential of CPDs-F@MIP decreased with increasing pH. Given that the pK a of SDS is 5.7–6.0 [59,60], it has a positive charge when pH is below 6. Thus, electrostatic repulsion between SDS and CPDs-F@MIP prevails over attraction, reducing the adsorption capacity and rendering electrostatic repulsion the dominant force in the adsorption process [61]. Between pH 6.0 and 9.0, SDS, exhibiting a relatively high negative charge, is rapidly adsorbed. Electrostatic attraction significantly contributes to the SDS adsorption onto the positively charged surface of CPDs-F@MIP. Beyond pH 9, the diminished charge on CPDs-F@MIP weakens the electrostatic force, thereby reducing the adsorption quantity [62].

In conclusion, CPDs-F@MIP, fabricated using the spherical functional monomer CPDs-F, exhibits a denser structure with reduced non-imprinted regions and precise imprinted sites. The polymerizable unit (−C=C) in CPDs-F can participate the polymerization process of imprinted material, and the recognition unit (hydroxyl group, imidazole ring and fluorine) can form hydrogen bonding with −NH, −NH2, and S=O groups in template molecule. This structure demonstrates heightened affinity and selectivity towards SDS in complex matrices, attributed to the synergistic interplay of hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π interactions (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SDS in actual samples and regeneration
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CPDs-F@MIP was used as an SPE adsorbent for the selective adsorption of SDS from complex environmental waters. As shown in Table S10, a standard curve was established using blank water as the solvent, with a linear range of 2 to 1000 μg L−1 and a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9993. The limit of detection (LOD), which represents the minimum detectable concentration of the analyte, was calculated based on a signal-to-noise ratio of three. In this method, the LOD was determined to be 0.2 μg L−1. To evaluate the applicability of the established CPDs-F@MIP-SPE-HPLC method for the selective adsorption of SDS in aqueous systems, six environmental water samples were selected as actual samples. The results listed in Table 4 indicate that SDS was not detected in any of the water samples. To further demonstrate the feasibility of the method, spike experiments were conducted on samples with three different concentrations: 10, 20, and 50 μg L−1. The recovery rates of SDS ranged from 91.79% to 111.32%. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the HPLC intensity of SDS significantly increased after the enrichment treatment, implying that CPDs-F@MIP effectively and selectively adsorbs SDS in environmental water. Moreover, the obtained chromatogram, compared to the original water sample, exhibited no apparent matrix interference peaks, indicating its high selectivity and anti-interference capability in complex environmental samples.

The most important criterion for the further application of adsorbents is reproducibility. In this work, a 10 mL sample of the SDS aqueous solution (1 mg L−1) was filtered via the self-made CPDs-F@MIP SPE column, after SDS adsorption, the CPDs-F@MIP adsorbent was regenerated with methanol (1 mL, containing 0.2% acetic acid, v/v) and subsequently with deionised water. The HPLC method was used to quantify the concentration of SDS in the effluent. In subsequent cycles, the regenerated CPDs-F@MIP adsorbent was reused for SDS adsorption. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the adsorption performance did not decrease significantly after each regeneration process throughout the cycling process. After 20 cycles, the SDS adsorption efficiency decreased by approximately 6.10%, indicating that CPDs-F@MIP possesses commendable reusability.

In this study, a novel spherical structural functional monomer, CPDs-F, was developed through the partial carbonisation of custom-designed 1-vinyl-3-hydroxyethylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ionic liquid, which incorporates polymeric units (C=C) and functional units (–F). Subsequently, using sulfadiazine sodium (SDS) as the template, polystyrene-divinylbenzene as the carrier, and N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide as the crosslinker, highly selective CPDs-F@MIP was prepared in water. The material was subjected to meticulous adsorption experiments and comprehensive structural characterisation. CPDs-F@MIP revealed exceptional adsorption selectivity, demonstrating a maximum adsorption capacity of 61.06 mg g−1 for SDS and an imprinting factor of 6.39. This notable selectivity can be attributed to the MIP synthesised from the novel spherical CPDs-F functional monomer, which exhibits a denser structure, minimised non-imprinted regions, and meticulous imprinted sites, demonstrating enhanced affinity and selectivity towards SDS in complex matrices through the synergistic effects of hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π interactions. The established CPDs-F@MIP-SPE-HPLC system was capable of recognising and removing SDS from six actual water samples, achieving high recoveries ranging from 91.79% to 111.32%. In addition, the potential for regeneration was demonstrated through at least 20 cycles.

This study presents the use of novel spherical functional monomers derived from CPDs for the fabrication of MIPs. This method features a straightforward and environmentally friendly preparation process. The use of these CPDs-derived monomers significantly enhances the selectivity of MIPs, indicating their substantial potential for various practical applications. This innovative approach marks a significant advancement in the field of high-selectivity adsorbents and will inspire novel ideas for the implementation of high-selectivity adsorbent construction.
